---
title: SASS and CSS
---

::: {.pale-blue}

**On this page we will:**

- Understand, in simple terms, what Sass and CSS are.
- See when you might use each in a Quarto site.
- Make changes using a `.scss` file and a `.css` file.

:::

When you customise the look of a Quarto site, you are changing how the **web page is styled**.

Web pages are styled with **CSS**, which stands for "Cascading Style Sheets". CSS is a simple styling language that tells the browser things like:

* "Make all main headings this colour".
* "Add extra space around this box".
* "Use this font for the body text".

There is also **Sass**, which you can think of as a **helper language for writing CSS**. Sass lets you define variables (for example, `$navbar-bg` for the navbar background colour). Quarto themes come with a set of Sass variables you can override, so you can change key theme‑level settings without writing lots of detailed rules. 

In practice in a Quarto site:

* Use **Sass** when want to change **theme-level settings** (and there is a Sass variable for it), such as overall background, text, or navbar colours.
* Use **CSS** when want to **add or adjust specific styles**, such as a custom class for a callout, extra spacing, or the look of a particular box or heading.

## Sass

You use Sass in Quarto by creating an `.scss` file and listing it under `theme:`.

Quarto HTML themes expose many Sass variables you can override (for example, `$body-bg`, `$body-color`, `$navbar-bg`). You can see a fuller list in the [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-themes.html#sass-variables).

Example SCSS theme file:

```scss
$body-bg: #fdf6e3;
$body-color: #002b36;
$navbar-bg: #d33682;
```

> Here the values like `#d33682` are **hex colour codes**. A hex code is a way of writing a colour using six characters after a `#`. You can find these by searching online, or can pick them from an image using a [hex colour picker](https://imagecolorpicker.com/).

Then in `_quarto.yml`:

```{.yaml}
format:
  html:
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
```

This says, start with `default` theme, then apply my overrides from `styles.scss`.

::: {.pale-blue}

**Task**:

* [ ] Create a `styles.scss` file with at least one Sass variable (for example, change `$navbar-bg` or `$body-bg`).  
* [ ] Add the SCSS file to your theme configuration in `_quarto.yml`.  
* [ ] Render the site and check the colour changes.
:::

## CSS

You use CSS in Quarto by creating a `.css` file and listing it under `format:` → `html:` → `css:` in `_quarto.yml` (or in an individual page's YAML):

```{.yaml}
format:
  html:
    css: custom.css
```

Inside `custom.css`, you write rules like:

```{.css}
h1, h2 {
  letter-spacing: 0.03em;
}

.pale-blue {
  background-color: #e6f2fa;
  color: black;
  border: 2px solid #9ecae1;
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 1.2em 1em 0.5em 1em;
  margin: 1.5em 0;
  position: relative;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(25, 118, 210, 0.15);
}
```

- The `h1, h2` rule changes the spacing between letters for level‑1 and level‑2 headings.  
- The `.pale-blue` rule controls how any element with class `pale-blue` is styled - this example is the blue boxes we used for tasks on this site.

::: {.pale-blue}

**Task:**

- [ ] Create a `custom.css` file in your project.  
- [ ] Add at least one simple rule (for example, change heading letter‑spacing, or adjust the `.pale-blue` box styling).  
- [ ] Add `css: custom.css` under `format: html:` in `_quarto.yml`.  
- [ ] Render the site and confirm that your CSS change appears.

:::
